ccel.title: NPNF-212. Leo the Great, Gregory the Great

title: To Theodorus, Physician.

source.title: NPNF-212. Leo the Great, Gregory the Great

verses.book: Register of the Epistles of St. Gregory the Great.
Register of the Epistles of St. Gregory the Great.
verses.text: What benefits I enjoy from Almighty God and my most serene lord the Emperor my tongue cannot fully express.

verses.book: Register of the Epistles of St. Gregory the Great.
verses.text: For these benefits what return is it in me to make, but to love their footsteps sincerely?

verses.book: Register of the Epistles of St. Gregory the Great.
verses.text: But, on account of my sins, by whose suggestion or counsel I know not, in the past year he has promulged such a law in his republic that whoso loves him sincerely must lament exceedingly.

verses.book: Register of the Epistles of St. Gregory the Great.
verses.text: I could not reply to this law at the time, being sick.

verses.book: Register of the Epistles of St. Gregory the Great.
verses.text: But I have just now offered some suggestions to my lord.

verses.book: Register of the Epistles of St. Gregory the Great.
verses.text: For he enjoins that it shall be lawful for no one to become a monk who has been engaged in any public employment, for no one who is a paymaster, or who has been marked in the hand, or enrolled among the soldiers, unless perchance his military service has been completed.

verses.book: Register of the Epistles of St. Gregory the Great.
verses.text: This law, as those say who are acquainted with old laws, Julian was the first to promulge, of whom we all know how opposed he was to God.

verses.book: Register of the Epistles of St. Gregory the Great.
verses.text: Now if our most serene lord has done this thing because perhaps many soldiers were becoming monks, and the army was decreasing, was it by the valour of soldiers that Almighty God subjugated to him the empire of the Persians?

verses.book: Register of the Epistles of St. Gregory the Great.
verses.text: Was it not only that his tears were heard, and that God, by an order which he knew not of, subdued to his empire the empire of the Persians?

verses.book: Register of the Epistles of St. Gregory the Great.
verses.text: Now it seems to me exceedingly hard that he should debar his soldiers from the service of Him who both gave him all and granted him to rule not only over soldiers but even over priests.

verses.book: Register of the Epistles of St. Gregory the Great.
verses.text: If his purpose is to save property from being lost, why might not those same monasteries into which soldiers have been received pay their debts, retaining the men only for monastic profession?

verses.book: Register of the Epistles of St. Gregory the Great.
verses.text: Since these things grieve me much, I have represented the matter to my lord.

verses.book: Register of the Epistles of St. Gregory the Great.
verses.text: But let your Glory take a favourable opportunity of offering him my representation privately.

verses.book: Register of the Epistles of St. Gregory the Great.
verses.text: For I am unwilling that it should be given publicly by my representative (responsalis), seeing that you who serve him familiarly can speak more freely and openly of what is for the good of his soul, since he is occupied with many things, and it is not easy to find his mind free from greater cares.

verses.book: Register of the Epistles of St. Gregory the Great.
verses.text: Do thou, then, glorious son, speak for Christ.

verses.book: Register of the Epistles of St. Gregory the Great.
verses.text: If thou art heard, it will be to the profit of the soul of thy aforesaid lord and of thine own.

verses.book: Register of the Epistles of St. Gregory the Great.
verses.text: But if thou art not heard, thou hast profited thine own soul only.
